title: Significant: Summation exchange and boundary conditions in Lemma lem:simple (iii) unverified

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
## Gap Description

In the proof of Lemma lem:simple (iii) -- that (T_eps p) boxplus_n (T_eps q) = T_eps^2(p boxplus_n q)
-- the solution performs a change of variables i' = i-m_1, j' = j-m_2, m = m_1+m_2
and then applies the Vandermonde identity:
  sum_{m_1=0}^m C(n,m_1)*C(n,m-m_1) = C(2n,m)

The claimed result is that the LHS equals (after collecting terms):
  1/(n!(n-k)!) * sum_{m=0}^k (-eps)^m * C(2n,m) * sum_{i'+j'=k-m} (n-i')!(n-j')! a_{i'} b_{j'}

## The Unverified Step

The original triple sum is over:
  {(i, j, m_1, m_2) : i+j=k, 0 <= m_1 <= min(i,n), 0 <= m_2 <= min(j,n)}

After changing variables i' = i-m_1, j' = j-m_2:
  {(i', j', m_1, m_2) : i'+j'=k-m, i'>=0, j'>=0, m_1>=0, m_2>=0, m=m_1+m_2}

BUT: there are implicit upper bounds m_1 <= n and m_2 <= n (since T_eps = (1-eps d/dx)^n
uses derivatives up to order n only). When m_1 > n, the binomial C(n,m_1) = 0 automatically,
so those terms vanish. The factoring of the (i', j') sum from the (m_1, m_2) sum requires
that the (i', j') range is INDEPENDENT of the split (m_1, m_2) for fixed m. This is true
because a_{i'} = 0 for i' > n, so the i' sum naturally terminates.

The solution states "changing variables ... and collecting" without verifying:
1. That i' + j' = k - m with i', j' >= 0 is the correct domain (i.e., a_{i'} = 0 for i' < 0).
2. That the Vandermonde identity applies with the correct upper limit (m <= k, not m > k).
3. That terms with m_1 > n are zero because C(n,m_1) = 0.

## Why This Matters

If the summation exchange is incorrect, the identity (T_eps p) boxplus_n (T_eps q) = T_eps^2(p boxplus_n q)
fails, and the entire simple-root reduction collapses. This identity is also used (without proof)
in the parenthetical statement about the heat operator at line 130-131 of solution.tex.

## Suggested Fix

Add an explicit sentence: "For m_1 > n, C(n,m_1) = 0 and for i' > n, a_{i'} = 0, so
all sums terminate at their natural upper bounds and the exchange is valid."
